title: LangChain BolyraAuthTool with structured output schema
description: Build a LangChain BaseTool subclass that wraps the Bolyra Python SDK. The tool should accept a peer's public signals, run proveHandshake() internally, and return a Pydantic model (BolyraAuthResult) with fields: verified: bool, nullifier: str, scopeCommitment: str, delegationDepth: int. This lets any LangChain agent chain gate sensitive tool calls behind mutual ZK auth with zero custom glue code. Include a BolyraAuthGuard runnable that short-circuits the chain if auth fails.

title: Map CrewAI task delegation to Delegation circuit scope narrowing
description: CrewAI's Task.delegate() currently has no cryptographic enforcement — any agent can claim any role. Build a BolyraCrewMiddleware that intercepts delegation events, maps CrewAI task permissions to Bolyra's 64-bit scope bitmask (e.g., bit 0 = read, bit 1 = write, bit 2 = financial), generates a Delegation proof with subset enforcement, and attaches the newScopeCommitment to the task context. Downstream agents must present a valid scope chain to access gated tools. This turns CrewAI's trust-based delegation into cryptographically enforced scope narrowing.

title: Python SDK with native snarkjs bridge and async proof generation
description: The Python SDK currently needs to shell out to snarkjs via subprocess, which is fragile and slow. Build a bolyra PyPI package that uses a bundled Node.js worker (via nodejs-bin) with a Unix socket IPC bridge, exposing async methods: create_identity(), enroll_human(), enroll_agent(), prove_handshake(), verify_handshake(), delegate(). Proof generation runs in a background thread so it doesn't block the LangChain/CrewAI event loop. Target: pip install bolyra && 8 lines to a verified handshake.

title: AutoGen GroupChat mutual auth middleware
description: AutoGen's GroupChat lets multiple agents converse freely with no identity verification — any agent can impersonate another. Build a BolyraGroupChatManager subclass that requires each agent to present a valid AgentPolicy proof on join and performs mutual handshake verification before the first message exchange. Store per-agent nullifiers in the chat context to detect if an agent's credential gets revoked mid-conversation. This is the first multi-agent framework to have cryptographic agent identity in group settings.

title: Framework-friendly error codes with recovery hints
description: Current contract reverts are opaque hex (e.g., StaleAgentRoot). For framework developers debugging agent pipelines, replace all revert strings with structured error codes (BOLYRA-001 through BOLYRA-015) and ship a companion errors.json mapping each code to: human-readable message, likely cause, and recovery action (e.g., BOLYRA-003: 'Agent Merkle root is stale — re-fetch the latest root from IdentityRegistry.agentRootHistory and regenerate the proof'). The Python and TS SDKs should catch these and raise typed exceptions with the recovery hint attached.

title: Multi-party handshake for agent marketplaces
description: The current handshake is 1-human-to-1-agent. Agentic commerce requires a human to authorize a primary agent that then orchestrates sub-agents (e.g., a shopping agent calling a payment agent calling a shipping agent). Add a 'session scope token' public output to HumanUniqueness that encodes the transaction context, and extend IdentityRegistry to accept a DAG of delegation proofs (not just a linear chain) within a single session. This unlocks multi-agent workflows like autonomous procurement, where 3-5 agents collaborate under one human's authority.

title: SDK credential lifecycle management (rotate, expire, upgrade)
description: The TypeScript SDK seed covers happy-path proving but ignores the 90% of developer time spent on credential lifecycle. Add enrollAgent() auto-renewal that re-enrolls before expiryTimestamp, a rotateOperatorKey() flow that atomically updates the agentTree leaf, and an upgradeScope() method that creates a new credential with expanded permissions requiring a fresh delegation proof. Without lifecycle primitives, every integrator will build broken ad-hoc solutions and blame the protocol.

title: Standardized financial scope bits for agentic payments
description: The permissionBitmask is generic but agentic commerce needs a canonical bit allocation for financial operations. Define bits 0-15 as the 'commerce tier': bit 0 = read-only price check, bit 1 = hold/reserve funds, bit 2 = spend up to $100, bit 3 = spend up to $10k (cumulative encoding already enforced). Publish this as a scope registry in the SDK and wire it into the Delegation circuit's subset check. This gives every agent framework a shared language for 'how much can this agent spend?' — the single question that blocks enterprise adoption.

title: Post-handshake verifiable action receipts
description: After a handshake, there is no protocol-level record of what the agent actually did within its authorized scope. Add a lightweight 'receipt' circuit that takes the scopeCommitment, an action hash (e.g., Poseidon of the API call payload), and the sessionNonce, and outputs a receipt commitment stored on-chain. This creates an auditable trail — 'agent X, authorized for scope Y, performed action Z' — without revealing the agent's identity. Essential for compliance in financial services and for dispute resolution in agentic marketplaces.

title: OpenAPI security scheme for Bolyra handshake auth
description: Define a custom OpenAPI 3.1 security scheme ('bolyraHandshake') that API providers can drop into their spec. The scheme specifies required scope bits, proof format (JSON with base64-encoded public signals + proof), and verification endpoint. Generate a Bolyra middleware for Express/FastAPI that reads the scheme from the OpenAPI doc and auto-verifies proofs. This is the adoption wedge: any API that adds 4 lines to their OpenAPI spec instantly supports authenticated agent access, and any SDK-equipped agent can call it without custom integration.

title: L2-to-L2 Merkle root relay via storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside the existing Base deployment, and implement a StorageProofRelay contract that reads agentTree/humanTree roots from Base's L1 state root using EIP-1186 storage proofs. Each L2 registry accepts cross-chain roots after verifying the storage proof against the L1 block hash available via the L2's precompile (ArbSys on Arbitrum, StateSender on Polygon). This eliminates trusted relayers and lets a proof generated against Base's tree be verified on Arbitrum or Polygon within one L1 finality window (~12 min).

title: Add root history buffer to humanTree matching agentTree
description: IdentityRegistry already maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree (agentRootHistory/agentRootExists), but humanTree has no equivalent — any new enrollment instantly stales in-flight human proofs. Add an identical uint256[30] humanRootHistory ring buffer with humanRootExists mapping, updated on every HumanEnrolled event. This is a prerequisite for cross-chain: root relay latency means stale roots are guaranteed on remote chains, so without a buffer the entire multi-chain architecture breaks.

title: Domain-separate nullifiers and nonces by chainId
description: Currently nullifierHash in both HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy circuits is computed without chain context (Poseidon2(scope, secret) and Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, ...)). If the same registry is deployed on multiple chains, a proof verified on Base can be replayed on Arbitrum since the nullifier is identical. Add chainId as a public input to all three circuits and include it in nullifier derivation: nullifier = Poseidon3(scope, secret, chainId) for humans, Poseidon4(..., chainId) for agents. The on-chain verifier passes block.chainid and checks it matches the public signal.

title: ERC-3668 (CCIP Read) off-chain verification gateway
description: Implement an ERC-3668 OffchainLookup gateway so that a contract on any EVM chain can verify a Bolyra handshake without deploying the full registry. The verifier contract on the target chain reverts with OffchainLookup pointing to a gateway URL, the gateway fetches the proof and root from the canonical chain (Base), and the callback verifies the SNARK proof plus a signed root attestation. This enables lightweight integration on chains where deploying the full Groth16+PLONK verifier set is too expensive (e.g., Polygon zkEVM with high verification gas).

title: Multi-chain provider abstraction in @bolyra/sdk
description: Add a ChainProvider interface to the planned TypeScript SDK that abstracts chain-specific details: RPC endpoints, registry contract addresses, root relay status, and chain-specific gas estimation. Ship with concrete providers for Base, Arbitrum, and Polygon. The SDK auto-selects the cheapest chain for verification, checks root freshness across chains, and handles chainId injection into circuit inputs. Developers call proveHandshake({chain: 'arbitrum'}) and the SDK handles the rest, including falling back to Base if the remote root is stale.

title: Delegatee Merkle inclusion proof requirement
description: The Delegation circuit accepts delegateeCredCommitment as a private input with zero verification that it corresponds to an enrolled entity in either humanTree or agentTree. An attacker can fabricate an arbitrary field element as delegateeCredCommitment, receive a valid delegation proof, and then use the resulting newScopeCommitment downstream. Fix: add a Merkle inclusion proof for the delegatee against agentTree (or a union root), with the verified root exposed as a public output for on-chain root-staleness checking.

title: Human tree root history buffer matching agent tree
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-slot root history ring buffer for agentTree (agentRootHistory + agentRootExists), but humanTree has no equivalent. The contract verifies humanMerkleRoot via humanTree.root directly, meaning any new enrollment immediately invalidates all in-flight human proofs. Add a parallel humanRootHistory[30] buffer with the same insert-on-enrollment and isValidHumanRoot() lookup pattern. Without this, the handshake protocol has a liveness failure under concurrent enrollment load.

title: Blinding salt in scope commitment to prevent bitmask grinding
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) is deterministic over a 64-bit bitmask space. An observer who knows a target's credentialCommitment (public after enrollment) can precompute all 2^64 scope commitments — but practically, real-world bitmasks occupy a much smaller space (likely <2^16 meaningful combinations), making brute-force trivial. Replace with Poseidon3(bitmask, credCommitment, blindingSalt) where blindingSalt is a private input, and update the Delegation circuit's chain-linking check to carry the salt through each hop.

title: Formal invariant property specifications for all three circuits
description: Write machine-checkable property specifications for each circuit targeting a Circom-aware formal verification tool (e.g., Picus, circom-mutator, or manual R1CS extraction + SMT solver). Key properties: (1) HumanUniqueness — nullifier uniqueness (same secret+scope always yields same nullifier, different secrets never collide), no under-constrained signals; (2) AgentPolicy — Num2Bits(64) range checks prevent field-wrapping on permissionBitmask and expiryTimestamp, bitmask subset check is sound (requiredScopeMask & permissionBitmask == requiredScopeMask); (3) Delegation — scope monotonicity (delegateeScope is strict subset of delegatorScope across any chain of hops), expiry narrowing (delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry). Deliver as a property spec file plus a CI-runnable verification script.

title: On-chain expiry freshness check for agent credentials
description: AgentPolicy.circom proves expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp inside the circuit, but the on-chain IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() does not verify that the currentTimestamp public signal is recent (e.g., within a tolerance of block.timestamp). A prover could use a stale currentTimestamp value far in the past to make an expired credential appear valid. Add a check in verifyHandshake: require(pubSignals[currentTimestampIdx] >= block.timestamp - MAX_CLOCK_DRIFT && pubSignals[currentTimestampIdx] <= block.timestamp + MAX_CLOCK_DRIFT). This closes a time-manipulation vector where the prover controls the timestamp input.

title: IETF Internet-Draft: Bolyra Core Protocol Specification
description: Author an Internet-Draft following RFC 7942 (Running Code) and using RFC 2119/8174 keywords. Sections: Introduction, Terminology (human/agent/delegatee/scope/nullifier), Protocol Overview (enrollment, handshake, delegation), Circuit Public Interface (inputs/outputs/semantics for all three circuits without implementation details), Wire Format (CBOR-encoded proof payloads with CDDL schema), On-Chain Verification Interface (ABI-level contract interface as normative), and Security Considerations (replay, linkability, root staleness, delegation chain depth). This is the foundational document that all interop work depends on — without it, independent implementations will diverge on semantics like nullifier derivation order or scope commitment structure.

title: W3C DID Method Specification: did:bolyra
description: Define a DID method binding per W3C DID Core 1.0. Method-specific identifier is the identity/credential commitment (base58btc-encoded). DID Document includes a verificationMethod of type EcdsaSecp256k1VerificationKey2019 for the operator key (agents) or Ed25519VerificationKey2020 mapped from BabyJubjub for humans, plus a BolyraProofService endpoint. Resolution algorithm: given did:bolyra:<commitment>, query the IdentityRegistry to confirm tree membership and non-revocation, then construct the DID Document. Also define a VerifiableCredential schema for handshake results (issuer = verifier, subject = DID pair, evidence = proof hashes). This bridges Bolyra into the existing decentralized identity ecosystem and enables interop with any W3C VC wallet.

title: Conformance Test Suite with JSON Test Vectors
description: Produce a machine-readable test suite (JSON) with 50+ vectors covering: valid handshake (human+agent proofs accepted), expired credential (expiryTimestamp < currentTimestamp, must reject), revoked human (nullifier in revocation set), stale agent root (root not in 30-entry history buffer), scope subset violation (delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope != 0), delegation chain at depths 1, 3, and 4 (4 must fail), nonce reuse (must reject), and the phantom delegatee attack (delegatee commitment not in any tree, must reject once fixed). Each vector specifies: circuit name, all public inputs/outputs, expected verdict (accept/reject), and failure reason code. This is essential for any second implementation to prove conformance without reverse-engineering the Circom source.

title: ABNF Grammar and CDDL Schema for Proof Payloads
description: Define machine-parseable grammars for all protocol messages: enrollment requests (human and agent), handshake proof bundles (paired human+agent proofs with session nonce), delegation tokens (chained scope commitments with per-hop proofs), and verification responses. Use CDDL (RFC 8610) for the binary CBOR encoding and ABNF (RFC 5234) for any text-based representations. Assign CBOR tags from the IANA range for Bolyra-specific types. This eliminates ambiguity in how proofs are serialized across implementations — currently the only wire format is implicit in the Solidity calldata ABI, which is insufficient for off-chain or cross-chain verification scenarios.

title: NIST SP 800-63B Authenticator Assurance Level Mapping
description: Produce a formal analysis mapping Bolyra's authentication factors to NIST AAL levels. The human side (EdDSA secret + Merkle membership) constitutes a single cryptographic authenticator factor; the agent side (operator EdDSA signature + model hash + permission bitmask) constitutes a bound multi-factor authenticator. Analyze whether the handshake protocol meets AAL2 requirements (two distinct factors, verifier impersonation resistance, replay resistance via nonce). Identify gaps: the current HumanUniqueness circuit is Phase 1 'proof of enrollment' without a second factor (e.g., biometric or PIN), which likely caps human-side assurance at AAL1. Document what Phase 4 behavioral enrollment would need to reach AAL2. This mapping is essential for credit union adoption where NCUA examiners will ask for NIST alignment.

title: Reuse Num2Bits between range checks and subset logic in Delegation
description: The Delegation circuit instantiates 4 separate Num2Bits(64) components for range checks on delegatorScope, delegateeScope, delegateeExpiry, and delegatorExpiry. The scope bits from the first two are then needed again for the subset check (delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0). By reusing the bit decompositions from the range check step directly in the subset logic, we eliminate 2 redundant Num2Bits(64) instantiations (~128 constraints) and reduce the risk of inconsistency between the range-checked bits and the bits used in subset verification. This is a correctness hardening: if a future refactor accidentally uses different bit arrays for range check vs subset check, soundness breaks.

title: Add delegatee Merkle inclusion proof to Delegation circuit
description: The Delegation circuit accepts delegateeCredCommitment as a private input but never proves it exists in any on-chain tree. An attacker can fabricate a credential commitment, receive a valid delegation, and use the resulting scopeCommitment downstream. Fix: add a BinaryMerkleRoot(MAX_DEPTH) component that proves delegateeCredCommitment is a leaf in agentTree, and output the computed root as a public signal for on-chain verification. This adds ~10k constraints (one Merkle path at depth 20) but closes a critical soundness gap. The contract must then check the output root against agentRootHistory.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment to prevent bitmask brute-force
description: scopeCommitment is currently Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment). Since the bitmask is only 64 bits, an observer who knows the public scopeCommitment and can guess credentialCommitment (it's a Merkle leaf) can brute-force all 2^64 bitmask values — feasible for sparse bitmasks with few bits set. Replace with Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt) where blindingSalt is a new private input. Cost: one extra Poseidon input slot (~200 constraints). The same salt must thread through the Delegation circuit's chain-linking logic: the delegator must prove knowledge of their salt when the verifier checks previousScopeCommitment.

title: Add humanTree root history buffer matching agentTree's 30-root ring
description: agentTree has a 30-root history buffer in IdentityRegistry so proofs generated against a recent-but-not-current root remain valid. humanTree has no such buffer — any new enrollment immediately invalidates all in-flight HumanUniqueness proofs. This is a liveness bug: in a system with frequent enrollments, human proofs will fail intermittently. Add a uint256[30] humanRootHistory ring buffer and mapping(uint256 => bool) humanRootExists, mirroring the agent side. No circuit changes needed — this is purely a contract fix — but from a circuit engineer's perspective the proof's humanMerkleRoot output must be checked against this buffer, not just the current root.

title: Replace EdDSA verify with Poseidon-based HMAC credential binding in AgentPolicy
description: EdDSA verification in AgentPolicy consumes ~30k constraints — over 60% of the circuit's budget. For the specific use case (operator binds a credential they created), full EdDSA is overkill. Alternative: the operator computes credentialMAC = Poseidon2(operatorSecret, credentialCommitment) off-chain and stores the MAC alongside the credential. The circuit proves knowledge of operatorSecret such that Poseidon2(operatorSecret, credentialCommitment) == credentialMAC (public input). This replaces EdDSA (~30k constraints) with one Poseidon hash (~250 constraints), dropping the total circuit from ~50k to ~20k constraints and cutting proving time roughly in half. Trade-off: the operator's public key is no longer embedded in the proof, so operator identity must be established via the Merkle leaf binding instead.

title: @bolyra/sdk: 8-line hello-world TypeScript core
description: Ship a unified @bolyra/sdk package with six ergonomic functions: createIdentity(), enrollHuman(), enrollAgent(), proveHandshake(), verifyHandshake(), and delegate(). Each function wraps snarkjs witness generation, proof creation, and on-chain submission behind a single async call with full TypeScript generics. The package must include a quickstart that achieves a verified human-agent handshake in under 8 lines of code, with auto-detection of circuit artifacts (wasm/zkey) via a resolve() helper that checks node_modules, local paths, and CDN fallback.

title: Typed error hierarchy with recovery hints
description: Define a BolyraError base class with typed subclasses: StaleRootError (includes which tree and how old the root is), RevokedIdentityError (includes the nullifier), ScopeMismatchError (includes required vs actual bitmask in human-readable form), ExpiredCredentialError (includes expiry delta), and ReplayError (includes the reused nonce). Each error carries a .hint string with the exact recovery action ('re-fetch the agent Merkle root—it rotated N blocks ago'). Map every Solidity revert in IdentityRegistry.sol to its SDK error class so developers never see raw hex reverts.

title: Fluent ScopeBuilder with cumulative-bit safety
description: The 64-bit permission bitmask with cumulative encoding (bit 4 implies bits 2+3) is a footgun for SDK consumers. Build a ScopeBuilder class with chainable methods like .allowRead().allowFinancial('10k').allowUnlimitedFinancial() that automatically sets prerequisite bits and throws at build-time if the result violates the cumulative invariant. Include .isSubsetOf(otherScope) and .toHumanReadable() for debugging delegation chains. This prevents the most common integration bug: setting bit 4 without bits 2+3 and getting a silent circuit constraint failure.

title: Python SDK + LangChain BolyraAuthTool
description: Publish a bolyra PyPI package that shells out to a bundled snarkjs binary for proof generation and uses web3.py for on-chain verification. Expose the same six-function API as the TypeScript SDK. Then ship a LangChain BaseTool subclass (BolyraAuthTool) that performs mutual human-agent authentication before sensitive tool calls, returning a BolyraSession object with .scope, .expiry, and .delegation_chain as structured output. This directly targets the LangChain/CrewAI audience who need agent auth without leaving Python.

title: npx bolyra-debug: interactive proof failure inspector
description: Build a CLI tool (npx bolyra-debug) that takes a failed proof or transaction hash, decodes the public signals, checks each potential failure mode (stale root? revoked? scope mismatch? expired? replay?), and prints a color-coded diagnostic with the exact fix. For circuit-level failures, it re-runs witness generation with verbose logging to show which constraint failed and maps it back to the named circuit step (e.g., 'STEP 2: Credential commitment — input permissionBitmask exceeds 64-bit range'). This turns a 2-hour debugging session into a 30-second diagnosis.

title: Enforce currentTimestamp freshness on-chain with bounded drift
description: The currentTimestamp public input is prover-supplied. A malicious prover can backdate it to keep expired credentials valid, or forward-date it to pre-generate proofs. The contract TODO mentions a 300s drift check (block.timestamp - 300 <= currentTimestamp <= block.timestamp) but it is unimplemented. Without this, the in-circuit LessThan(64) expiry check is bypassable. Implement the on-chain freshness window in both AgentPolicyVerifier.verifyProof() and DelegationRegistry.submitDelegation(), reverting with TimestampOutOfRange if the prover's timestamp deviates from block.timestamp by more than 300 seconds.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment against bitmask brute-force
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment). The permissionBitmask is only 64 bits wide with cumulative encoding, meaning there are far fewer than 2^64 valid bitmask values — likely under 2^10 plausible combinations. An observer can brute-force the bitmask by hashing each candidate against known credential commitments (which are public tree leaves). Fix: change to Poseidon3(bitmask, credCmt, blindingSalt) where blindingSalt is a 254-bit private random input. Update both AgentPolicy.circom (scope commitment output) and Delegation.circom (scope commitment chain linking). This breaks scope commitment linkability without breaking the delegation chain verification logic.

title: Prevent cross-scope identity linkage via nullifier algebraic correlation
description: HumanUniqueness nullifier = Poseidon2(scope, secret) where secret is the EdDSA private scalar. If a human participates in two different scopes, both nullifiers share the same secret input. While Poseidon is believed to be a PRF, the nullifier scheme lacks domain separation and an internal nullifier trapdoor (Semaphore v4 uses a dedicated nullifier secret distinct from the signing key). If Poseidon's PRF assumption weakens, an adversary observing nullifiers across scopes could link them to the same identity. Add a dedicated nullifierSecret (derived as Poseidon(secret, 'nullifier') during enrollment) and compute nullifier = Poseidon2(scope, nullifierSecret). This adds ~250 constraints and provides defense-in-depth against future cryptanalytic advances.

title: Replace stub DelegationVerifier with real PLONK verifier and add proof-of-nothing test
description: DelegationVerifier.sol is documented as a testing stub that accepts non-zero proofs. If this contract is deployed without regeneration from trusted setup, any user can forge delegation proofs with arbitrary scope commitments. This is a deployment-time critical vulnerability. Build a CI gate: (1) compile Delegation.circom with snarkjs, (2) export the Solidity verifier, (3) diff against the deployed contract bytecode, (4) fail the pipeline if they diverge. Additionally, add a 'proof-of-nothing' negative test that submits a structurally valid but semantically invalid proof (wrong witness) and asserts revert.

title: Add domain separator to EdDSA signed messages in AgentPolicy and Delegation
description: In AgentPolicy, the operator signs the raw credentialCommitment hash. In Delegation, the delegator signs Poseidon4(scopeCommitment, delegateeCredCommitment, delegateeScope, delegateeExpiry). Neither includes a domain separator tag, creating cross-circuit signature replay risk: a signature valid in AgentPolicy could potentially be replayed in Delegation if the message happens to collide (e.g., a crafted 5-input Poseidon output matching a 4-input Poseidon output). Prepend a unique domain tag to each signed message: AgentPolicy signs Poseidon2(AGENT_DOMAIN_TAG, credentialCommitment), Delegation signs Poseidon2(DELEGATION_DOMAIN_TAG, currentMessage). Tags should be circuit-specific constants (e.g., hash of circuit name).
